enrichment.short_description: Openfire is a free, open-source real-time collaboration server built on the XMPP protocol, used to run self-hosted instant messaging and group chat for organizations that want full control over their data.
enrichment.seo_title: Openfire Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Openfire is a free, open-source XMPP chat server for self-hosted team messaging. See features, pricing, pros and cons, and top alternatives for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Openfire is a free, open-source real-time collaboration server for instant messaging and group chat, built on the Extensible Messaging and Presence Protocol (XMPP), the only widely adopted open standard for chat interoperability. Its codebase originated at Jive Software around 2002 as 'Jive Messenger,' was renamed 'Wildfire' in 2005, and became 'Openfire' in 2007 after a trademark dispute. Jive handed the project fully to the community in 2008 and continued hosting it until 2016, when the Ignite Realtime Foundation, a Dutch non-profit (stichting), was formally established to steward Openfire and its sibling projects (such as the Smack and Spark clients). Written in Java and licensed under the Apache License 2.0, Openfire is completely free with no paid tier, no usage limits, and no vendor lock-in; organizations self-host it on their own infrastructure. As of mid-2026 the project has surpassed 10 million total downloads and continues active development, with the latest stable release (5.1.1) adding SASL channel binding to prevent man-in-the-middle attacks, expanded database support (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, MariaDB, Firebird, and CockroachDB), and compatibility with modern Java runtimes. Key differentiators include its mature plugin architecture (monitoring/archiving, REST API, LDAP/Active Directory integration, clustering for high availability), a web-based admin console, multi-user chat (MUC) rooms, and strong security defaults suited to regulated or privacy-conscious environments such as government, education, and enterprise IT. Because it is standards-based XMPP, Openfire interoperates with a wide range of third-party clients (Spark, Conversations, Gajim, Pidgin, Monal, and others) rather than shipping a single proprietary client. It targets IT teams and developers who want a self-hosted, auditable alternative to SaaS chat platforms like Slack or Microsoft Teams, or who need XMPP specifically for IoT device messaging, presence-based systems, or federated real-time communication. Support comes through community forums and documentation rather than a commercial vendor, though a handful of third-party consultancies offer paid Openfire support and hosting. Its closest alternatives are other self-hosted XMPP servers (ejabberd, Prosody, Tigase) and, for teams not tied to XMPP specifically, Matrix-based servers like Synapse or Element Server Suite, as well as commercial team-chat SaaS products such as Slack, Mattermost, and Rocket.Chat.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Openfire is a real-time collaboration (RTC) server that implements the XMPP protocol to power instant messaging, presence, and group chat for organizations that prefer to run their own infrastructure rather than rely on a hosted SaaS chat provider.

Originally built by Jive Software in the early 2000s, Openfire has been community-owned since 2008 and is now governed by the Ignite Realtime Foundation, a Dutch non-profit created in 2016 specifically to keep Openfire and related XMPP tooling independent and open source.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Openfire's core strength is its plugin ecosystem, which adds capabilities like message archiving and monitoring, a REST API for automation, LDAP or Active Directory-backed authentication, and clustering for high-availability deployments.

Because it speaks standard XMPP, Openfire works with a broad range of third-party clients on desktop and mobile, and it supports multi-user chat rooms, presence, and federation with other XMPP servers out of the box.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Openfire is entirely free under the Apache License 2.0. There are no paid tiers, per-user fees, or usage caps from the project itself; the only ongoing cost is the infrastructure and staff time needed to self-host it.
Organizations that want commercial support beyond the community forums typically contract with third-party consultancies rather than a single official vendor, since Openfire has no owning company.

features.title: XMPP-based messaging
features.description: Implements the open XMPP (Jabber) standard for instant messaging, presence, and group chat, ensuring interoperability with many third-party clients.

features.title: Plugin architecture
features.description: Extend the server with plugins for monitoring and archiving, a REST API, LDAP/Active Directory authentication, and more without modifying core code.

features.title: Multi-user chat rooms
features.description: Built-in MUC support for group conversations, moderated rooms, and persistent chat history.

features.title: Clustering and high availability
features.description: Supports clustered deployments so large organizations can scale Openfire across multiple nodes.

features.title: Flexible database support
features.description: Works with MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, MariaDB, Firebird, and CockroachCB for backend storage.

features.title: Security hardening
features.description: Includes SASL channel binding, TLS encryption, and certificate identity verification to guard against man-in-the-middle attacks.

features.title: Web-based admin console
features.description: A browser-based console for user management, diagnostics, DNS checks, and Docker health checks.

pros: Completely free and open source under the Apache License
Over two decades of production maturity and 10 million-plus downloads
Extensible through a wide plugin ecosystem
Self-hosted, giving organizations full control over chat data
Interoperable with many standard XMPP clients
cons: Requires technical staff to deploy, secure, and maintain
Admin console UI feels dated compared to modern SaaS tools
No official managed cloud/SaaS offering from the project itself
No single vendor for commercial enterprise support
End users need a separate XMPP client rather than a built-in unified app

faqs.question: Is Openfire free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Openfire is fully open source under the Apache License 2.0 with no paid tiers or usage limits; you only pay for the infrastructure you run it on.

faqs.question: What protocol does Openfire use?
faqs.answer: Openfire is built on XMPP (also known as Jabber), an open, federated messaging protocol widely used for chat and presence.

faqs.question: Can Openfire scale to large organizations?
faqs.answer: Yes, Openfire supports clustering for high availability and has been used in deployments with extensive PubSub nodes and large user bases.

faqs.question: What chat clients work with Openfire?
faqs.answer: Any standard XMPP client works, including Spark, Conversations, Gajim, Pidgin, and Monal, since Openfire follows the open XMPP specification.

faqs.question: Who maintains Openfire today?
faqs.answer: The Ignite Realtime Foundation, a Dutch non-profit established in 2016, oversees Openfire's development after Jive Software handed the project to the community.

faqs.question: Does Openfire support encryption?
faqs.answer: Openfire supports TLS for transport encryption and SASL channel binding; end-to-end encryption options like OMEMO are available through compatible clients and plugins.

faqs.question: Where can I get support for Openfire?
faqs.answer: Support is primarily community-driven through the Ignite Realtime forums and documentation, with some third-party consultancies offering paid support.

use_cases.title: Enterprise internal chat
use_cases.description: Companies that need private, self-hosted team messaging with full data ownership deploy Openfire as an alternative to SaaS chat tools.

use_cases.title: IoT and device messaging
use_cases.description: Openfire's XMPP foundation and presence model make it suitable for machine-to-machine messaging and IoT device communication.

use_cases.title: Education and large-campus chat
use_cases.description: Universities and large institutions use Openfire to run internal messaging platforms without relying on third-party cloud services.

enrichment.short_description: openHAB is a free, open-source home automation platform that runs locally on your own hardware and integrates with over 400 technologies to control smart-home devices without relying on the cloud.
enrichment.company_size: Non-profit foundation (openHAB Foundation e.V.) with 200-plus volunteer contributors
enrichment.seo_title: openHAB Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: openHAB is a free, open-source, privacy-first home automation platform. See its features, pricing, pros and cons, and how it compares to alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: openHAB (open Home Automation Bus) is a vendor- and technology-agnostic, open-source home automation platform started in 2010 by Kai Kreuzer. In 2013 its core functionality was contributed to the Eclipse Foundation as Eclipse SmartHome, and the project is now stewarded by the openHAB Foundation e.V., a registered non-profit association whose mission is to promote free and open-source software in smart-home technology and keep the platform free of commercial lock-in. Written in Java and built on Apache Karaf, openHAB is 100 percent open source and free of charge, with development driven by a community of more than 200 contributors. Its defining differentiator is a local-first, privacy-focused architecture: openHAB runs entirely on the user's own hardware (a Raspberry Pi, NAS, Docker container, or a full server on Linux, macOS, or Windows) and functions without any mandatory cloud service, meaning smart-home data stays on the user's premises. Through a pluggable 'bindings' architecture it connects to more than 400 technologies and thousands of individual devices, spanning protocols like Zigbee, Z-Wave, MQTT, and KNX, as well as commercial ecosystems. A built-in rule engine lets users design automations using time-based and event-based triggers, scripts, notifications, and voice control, and optional cloud connectors allow integration with Google Assistant, Amazon Alexa, and Apple HomeKit for those who want it. openHAB targets DIY smart-home enthusiasts, privacy-conscious homeowners, and system integrators who need a vendor-neutral automation layer that will not be discontinued or paywalled by a single company, distinguishing it from commercial platforms and from home-automation software backed by a single hardware vendor. Its closest open-source alternative is Home Assistant, which has a larger mainstream user base and a more polished onboarding experience, while openHAB is often preferred by users who want deep protocol-level integration flexibility and a foundation-governed, vendor-neutral roadmap. Because there is no company selling openHAB, there are no pricing tiers or paid plans; support comes from the community forum, documentation, and the non-profit openHAB Foundation, which some hardware and software vendors join as paying organizational members to help fund development.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: openHAB is an open-source home automation platform designed to unify smart-home devices from many different vendors under a single, self-hosted control layer, without requiring any cloud service to function.
Started in 2010 by Kai Kreuzer and now governed by the non-profit openHAB Foundation e.V., openHAB is developed by a community of 200-plus contributors and remains free and vendor-neutral by design.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: openHAB connects to more than 400 technologies and thousands of devices through its pluggable binding architecture, covering protocols like Zigbee, Z-Wave, MQTT, and KNX alongside many commercial device ecosystems.

A flexible rule engine supports time- and event-based automation, scripting, notifications, and voice control, while optional cloud connectors add compatibility with Google Assistant, Amazon Alexa, and Apple HomeKit for users who want it.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: openHAB is entirely free and open source; there is no paid tier, subscription, or licensing fee for the software itself.

Users only incur the cost of the hardware they run it on (such as a Raspberry Pi or small server); the openHAB Foundation is funded through voluntary organizational memberships rather than end-user fees.

features.title: 400-plus integrations
features.description: Pluggable bindings connect openHAB to over 400 technologies and thousands of individual smart-home devices.

features.title: Local-first, cloud-optional
features.description: Runs entirely on your own hardware and works without any mandatory cloud service, keeping data private at home.

features.title: Powerful rule engine
features.description: Design automations using time-based and event-based triggers, scripts, notifications, and voice commands.

features.title: Cross-platform deployment
features.description: Runs on Linux, macOS, Windows, Raspberry Pi, Docker, and Synology NAS devices.

features.title: Voice assistant integration
features.description: Optional connectors for Google Assistant, Amazon Alexa, and Apple HomeKit.

features.title: Mobile apps
features.description: Native Android and iOS apps for remote control and dashboard access.

features.title: Vendor-neutral architecture
features.description: Governed by a non-profit foundation rather than a single commercial vendor, avoiding lock-in.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paid tiers
Runs locally with no mandatory cloud dependency, protecting privacy
One of the largest device and protocol integration libraries available
Governed by a non-profit foundation, avoiding vendor lock-in
Active, long-running community with 200-plus contributors
cons: Steeper learning curve than consumer-friendly smart-home apps
User interface is less polished than some commercial competitors
Many bindings require manual configuration rather than plug-and-play setup
Documentation quality varies across the large add-on ecosystem
Requires ongoing self-hosting and maintenance effort

faqs.question: Is openHAB free?
faqs.answer: Yes, openHAB is 100 percent open source and free to use; you only pay for the hardware you run it on.

faqs.question: Does openHAB require an internet connection or cloud service?
faqs.answer: No, openHAB runs entirely on local hardware and does not require any cloud service to function, though optional cloud connectors are available.

faqs.question: How does openHAB compare to Home Assistant?
faqs.answer: Both are open-source, self-hosted automation platforms; openHAB is known for deep protocol-level integration flexibility and foundation governance, while Home Assistant is often seen as having a more polished onboarding experience.

faqs.question: What hardware do I need to run openHAB?
faqs.answer: openHAB can run on a Raspberry Pi, a NAS like Synology, a Docker container, or a standard Linux, macOS, or Windows machine.

faqs.question: How many devices does openHAB support?
faqs.answer: openHAB integrates with over 400 technologies and thousands of individual devices through its add-on binding system.

faqs.question: Who governs the openHAB project?
faqs.answer: The openHAB Foundation e.V., a registered non-profit association, stewards the project alongside a community of 200-plus volunteer contributors.

faqs.question: Can openHAB work with Alexa or Google Assistant?
faqs.answer: Yes, optional cloud connectors let openHAB integrate with Google Assistant, Amazon Alexa, and Apple HomeKit.

use_cases.title: DIY smart home automation
use_cases.description: Hobbyists build custom automation setups that unify devices from many vendors under one local control system.

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious home automation
use_cases.description: Households wanting to avoid sending smart-home data to third-party clouds run openHAB entirely on local hardware.

use_cases.title: Custom integration projects
use_cases.description: System integrators use openHAB's binding architecture to build tailored automation solutions across mixed-vendor device fleets.

enrichment.short_description: OpenKM is a Spanish document management and enterprise content management platform offering a free open-source Community Edition alongside paid Professional and Cloud editions with workflow, records management, and support.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Community Edition); Professional and Cloud plans are custom-quoted, with third-party estimates starting around $30/user/month
enrichment.seo_title: OpenKM Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenKM is a document management and ECM platform with a free open-source edition plus paid Professional and Cloud plans. Compare features, pricing, and pros/cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OpenKM is a document management system (DMS) and enterprise content management (ECM) platform developed by OpenKM, a Spanish software company headquartered in Palma de Mallorca, Spain. Founded in 2005, the company built its product around an open-source core, and today OpenKM offers three tiers: a free, self-hosted Community Edition released under an open-source license for organizations comfortable with community forum support and no official vendor backing; a paid Professional Edition with vendor support and additional enterprise features, with third-party estimates placing entry pricing around 30 US dollars per user per month; and a Cloud edition aimed at small and mid-sized businesses that want implementation and hosting handled for them, priced on request. OpenKM's core capabilities include document versioning, metadata and taxonomy management, full-text search with OCR, a workflow and business process automation engine built on jBPM, digital signature support, records management (RM) features for regulatory and compliance use cases, and REST APIs plus mobile apps for integration with other business systems. The company has grown an international presence with regional offices and partners across Italy, Poland, Serbia, the Middle East, France, and the United States, while keeping software development centered in its Palma de Mallorca headquarters; it employs roughly 11 to 50 people. OpenKM differentiates itself from larger ECM vendors like OpenText, M-Files, and SharePoint by offering a genuinely free and open-source starting point alongside a self-hosting option, appealing to legal, healthcare, government, and educational organizations that need document control and compliance features without committing to a large SaaS-only contract from day one. Its main tradeoffs are less transparent list pricing for the paid tiers (requiring a sales conversation), an admin interface that some reviewers describe as dated relative to newer cloud-native DMS tools, and no official support for the free Community Edition beyond public forums. It competes with other open-source and semi-open ECM platforms such as Alfresco, Nuxeo, and LogicalDOC, as well as commercial cloud document management tools like Box and DocuWare, for organizations that prioritize on-premises control, records management, and lower total cost of ownership over a fully managed SaaS experience.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenKM is a document management and enterprise content management platform built by a Spanish software company founded in 2005 and headquartered in Palma de Mallorca.
It is offered as a free, open-source Community Edition for self-hosting, plus paid Professional and Cloud editions that add vendor support, hosting, and additional enterprise capabilities.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: OpenKM covers the core DMS/ECM feature set: document versioning, metadata and taxonomy management, OCR-powered full-text search, digital signatures, and records management for regulatory compliance.

A built-in workflow and business process automation engine based on jBPM lets organizations automate approval chains and document lifecycles, while REST APIs and mobile apps support integration with existing business systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Community Edition is free to download, self-host, and use, though it comes without official vendor support beyond public community forums.

The Professional and Cloud editions are priced on a custom-quote basis after contacting OpenKM's sales team; third-party estimates place Professional pricing around 30 US dollars per user per month, with Cloud pricing covering hosting and implementation.

features.title: Document versioning and metadata
features.description: Track document revisions and organize content with customizable metadata and taxonomy structures.

features.title: OCR and full-text search
features.description: Index scanned documents with OCR and search across full document content, not just titles or tags.

features.title: Workflow automation (jBPM)
features.description: Automate document approval chains and business processes with a built-in workflow engine.

features.title: Records management
features.description: Manage retention schedules and regulatory compliance for organizations with formal records-management requirements.

features.title: Digital signature support
features.description: Sign documents digitally within the platform for legal and audit purposes.

features.title: REST API and integrations
features.description: Connect OpenKM to other business systems via REST APIs for custom integrations.

features.title: Mobile access
features.description: Mobile apps let users browse, search, and manage documents on the go.

pros: Free, open-source Community Edition available for self-hosting
Strong workflow and business process automation via jBPM
Purpose-built records management features for compliance-heavy industries
Scales from small teams to large enterprise deployments
International presence with localized support in multiple regions
cons: Professional and Cloud pricing is not published and requires a sales quote
Community Edition has no official vendor support, only forums
Admin interface feels dated compared to newer cloud-native DMS tools
Workflow configuration has a learning curve for non-technical users
Smaller third-party app marketplace than mainstream competitors like Box

faqs.question: Is OpenKM free?
faqs.answer: The Community Edition is free and open source for self-hosting, while the Professional and Cloud editions are paid and require a custom quote.

faqs.question: How much does OpenKM Professional cost?
faqs.answer: OpenKM does not publish list pricing; third-party estimates put Professional pricing at around 30 US dollars per user per month, but exact pricing depends on a sales quote.

faqs.question: What is the difference between OpenKM Community and Professional?
faqs.answer: The Community Edition is free but unsupported beyond public forums, while Professional adds vendor support and additional enterprise features for a paid subscription.

faqs.question: Where is OpenKM based?
faqs.answer: OpenKM is headquartered in Palma de Mallorca, Spain, and was founded in 2005.

faqs.question: Does OpenKM support workflow automation?
faqs.answer: Yes, OpenKM includes a business process automation engine built on jBPM for automating document approval chains and workflows.

faqs.question: Can OpenKM handle records management and compliance?
faqs.answer: Yes, OpenKM includes records management (RM) features designed for regulatory and compliance-driven document retention.

faqs.question: Does OpenKM offer a cloud-hosted option?
faqs.answer: Yes, OpenKM Cloud is available for organizations that want hosting and implementation handled by OpenKM, priced on request.

use_cases.title: Legal and compliance document management
use_cases.description: Law firms and compliance teams use OpenKM's records management and audit features to manage sensitive documents.

use_cases.title: Enterprise records management
use_cases.description: Large organizations use OpenKM to enforce retention schedules and regulatory document lifecycles across departments.

use_cases.title: Educational institution document control
use_cases.description: Universities and schools use OpenKM to manage administrative records, student files, and institutional documents.

enrichment.short_description: OpenLLMetry is a free, open-source SDK built on OpenTelemetry that adds observability and tracing to LLM applications with a single line of code, created by Traceloop and now backed by ServiceNow.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (SDK); optional Traceloop hosted platform has a free tier with paid plans custom-priced
enrichment.company_size: 11-50 employees (Traceloop, acquired by ServiceNow in March 2026)
enrichment.seo_title: OpenLLMetry Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenLLMetry is a free, open-source OpenTelemetry-based SDK for LLM app observability from Traceloop. See features, pricing, and top alternatives for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OpenLLMetry is an open-source software development kit that extends OpenTelemetry, the industry-standard observability framework, to add tracing and monitoring specifically for large language model (LLM) applications. It was created by Traceloop, a company founded in 2022 in Tel Aviv, Israel, by CEO Nir Gazit and CTO Gal Kleinman. Traceloop raised a 6.1 million dollar seed round in 2025 led by Sorenson Capital and Ibex Investors, with participation from Y Combinator, Samsung NEXT, and Grand Ventures, for a total of roughly 6.6 million dollars raised, before being acquired by ServiceNow in March 2026 in a deal reported at 60 to 80 million dollars to power ServiceNow's AI Control Tower governance platform; OpenLLMetry itself remains open source and the team has committed to continuing to contribute to it. Released under the Apache 2.0 license, OpenLLMetry lets developers instrument LLM applications with a single line of code to automatically capture traces of calls to providers like OpenAI and Anthropic, vector database queries, and agent workflows, without vendor lock-in, since it exports standard OpenTelemetry data compatible with more than 25 observability backends including Datadog, Grafana, and Honeycomb. As of 2026 it reports over 500,000 monthly installs, 50,000 weekly active SDK users, and more than 60 open-source contributors, with adoption from organizations including IBM and Microsoft. Traceloop also offers an optional hosted platform (built on top of OpenLLMetry data) with a 'Free Forever' tier covering up to 50,000 spans per month, up to 5 seats, and 24-hour data retention, including a monitoring dashboard, evaluation dashboard, CI/CD integration for prompt testing, and prompt management; an Enterprise plan adds unlimited seats, custom retention, SOC 2 compliance, on-premise deployment, and dedicated support, priced on request. OpenLLMetry targets AI engineering teams, ML platform teams, and enterprises building LLM-powered products who need visibility into cost, latency, and failure modes of AI systems, and who want an observability layer that will not lock them into a single proprietary vendor. Its closest alternatives include LangSmith, Langfuse, Helicone, Arize Phoenix, and Datadog's LLM observability features, with OpenLLMetry's key differentiator being its open, OpenTelemetry-native foundation versus competitors' more proprietary tracing formats.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenLLMetry is an open-source SDK that brings observability to LLM applications by extending OpenTelemetry, letting developers trace model calls, agent behavior, and vector database queries with minimal code changes.
It was built by Traceloop, a Tel Aviv startup founded in 2022 and acquired by ServiceNow in March 2026, though OpenLLMetry itself remains open source and independently usable.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: A single line of instrumentation code automatically traces calls to LLM providers such as OpenAI and Anthropic, along with vector database and agent framework activity.

Because it is built on OpenTelemetry, trace data exports to more than 25 observability backends, avoiding vendor lock-in, while the optional Traceloop platform adds monitoring dashboards, evaluation tooling, and prompt management.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The OpenLLMetry SDK itself is free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license, with no cost to instrument and export traces to a compatible backend.
Traceloop's optional hosted platform offers a free tier for up to 50,000 spans per month and 5 seats, with an Enterprise plan for larger teams priced on request.

features.title: One-line instrumentation
features.description: Add full LLM application tracing with a single line of code, no manual span instrumentation required.

features.title: OpenTelemetry-native
features.description: Built directly on OpenTelemetry standards, exporting data compatible with 25-plus observability backends.

features.title: Multi-provider LLM support
features.description: Automatically traces calls to major LLM providers including OpenAI and Anthropic.

features.title: Vendor-neutral data export
features.description: No lock-in to a single observability vendor since traces follow open OpenTelemetry semantic conventions.

features.title: Prompt management (via Traceloop)
features.description: Optional hosted platform adds prompt versioning and management tools.

features.title: Evaluation dashboards (via Traceloop)
features.description: Track LLM output quality and run evaluations against production traces.

features.title: CI/CD integration
features.description: Integrate prompt and trace testing into CI/CD pipelines for safer LLM app deployments.

pros: Fully open source and free under the Apache 2.0 license
Extremely fast to integrate with one-line instrumentation
Vendor-neutral OpenTelemetry foundation avoids lock-in
Large and fast-growing adoption with 500,000-plus monthly installs
Backed by ServiceNow's resources following the 2026 acquisition
cons: Full monitoring and evaluation platform requires a paid Traceloop plan beyond free-tier limits
Free tier data retention is limited to 24 hours
Enterprise pricing for the hosted platform is not publicly listed
Relatively young product line launched in 2022
Post-acquisition roadmap integration with ServiceNow introduces some uncertainty for standalone users

faqs.question: Is OpenLLMetry free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the OpenLLMetry SDK is open source under the Apache 2.0 license and free to use; only Traceloop's optional hosted platform has paid tiers beyond its free plan.

faqs.question: What is the difference between OpenLLMetry and Traceloop?
faqs.answer: OpenLLMetry is the open-source SDK for instrumenting LLM applications, while Traceloop is the company that built it and offers an optional hosted platform for visualizing and managing that trace data.

faqs.question: Which LLM providers does OpenLLMetry support?
faqs.answer: OpenLLMetry automatically traces calls to major LLM providers such as OpenAI and Anthropic, along with vector databases and agent frameworks.

faqs.question: Is OpenLLMetry based on OpenTelemetry?
faqs.answer: Yes, OpenLLMetry extends OpenTelemetry, the industry-standard observability framework, so its trace data works with more than 25 existing observability backends.

faqs.question: Who acquired Traceloop?
faqs.answer: ServiceNow acquired Traceloop in March 2026 to power its AI Control Tower governance platform, while committing to keep OpenLLMetry open source.

faqs.question: Can I self-host OpenLLMetry data?
faqs.answer: Yes, since OpenLLMetry exports standard OpenTelemetry data, you can route it to any self-hosted or third-party observability backend rather than using Traceloop's hosted platform.

faqs.question: Who is OpenLLMetry for?
faqs.answer: It is built for AI engineering and ML platform teams that need visibility into the cost, latency, and reliability of LLM-powered applications.

use_cases.title: Debugging LLM application behavior
use_cases.description: AI engineering teams use OpenLLMetry to trace and debug unexpected LLM outputs, latency spikes, and failures in production.

use_cases.title: Enterprise AI governance
use_cases.description: Enterprises adopting AI at scale use OpenLLMetry traces to feed governance and compliance workflows.

use_cases.title: Rapid observability for AI startups
use_cases.description: Startups building LLM-powered products add OpenLLMetry to quickly gain tracing and cost visibility without building custom instrumentation.

enrichment.short_description: Apache Openmeetings is a free, open-source web conferencing platform offering HTML5 video meetings, screen sharing, whiteboards, and document collaboration, governed as a top-level Apache Software Foundation project.
enrichment.company_size: Apache Software Foundation volunteer project (no fixed company size)
enrichment.headquarters: United States (Apache Software Foundation, a distributed volunteer organization)
languages: and more than 20 other localized languages
enrichment.seo_title: Apache Openmeetings Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Apache Openmeetings is a free, open-source video conferencing and whiteboard platform. See its features, self-hosting requirements, and alternatives for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Apache Openmeetings is a free, open-source web conferencing platform that provides HTML5 audio and video meetings, screen sharing, multi-user whiteboards, collaborative document editing, and other groupware tools. Development began in 2007 under creator Sebastian Wagner, the project opened up to outside contributors in 2009, and from 2011 onward the majority of active development and technical support shifted to a team based in Russia. The project entered the Apache Incubator in December 2011 and graduated to become a top-level Apache Software Foundation (ASF) project in December 2012, meaning it is governed by the ASF's open, consensus-based process rather than by a single company, and is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 with no cost to download, deploy, or use. Technically, Openmeetings relies on the Kurento media server for real-time audio/video processing and is built with HTML5, avoiding the Flash dependency of older conferencing tools. Its feature set spans meeting recording with MP4 export, calendar-based scheduling with automatic invitations, a per-room file explorer with drag-and-drop uploads, polls and voting with results visualization, private messaging, granular moderator permission controls, and enterprise integration options including LDAP/ADS authentication, OAuth2 login, and REST and SOAP APIs for embedding conferencing into external websites or intranets. Because it has no owning company, Openmeetings has no official pricing tiers; organizations self-host it on their own servers (or via third-party hosting providers) and can optionally purchase commercial support from independent consultancies rather than from the ASF itself. It is best suited for educational institutions running online classes, organizations that need to keep video conferencing data entirely in-house for privacy, compliance, or sovereignty reasons, and developers who want to white-label or embed video conferencing into their own products via its APIs. Its main competitors are other self-hosted, open-source conferencing tools like BigBlueButton (which is more explicitly focused on e-learning) and Jitsi Meet, as well as commercial SaaS platforms such as Zoom, Google Meet, and Microsoft Teams for organizations that do not require self-hosting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Apache Openmeetings is a free, open-source web conferencing and collaboration platform offering video meetings, whiteboards, and document collaboration tools, governed as a top-level Apache Software Foundation project since December 2012.

Development began in 2007, and after years of community contribution the project achieved ASF top-level status, ensuring it is maintained through an open, vendor-neutral governance process rather than by a single company.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Openmeetings offers HTML5 audio and video conferencing, screen sharing, meeting recording with MP4 export, and a multi-whiteboard tool that supports importing documents for real-time collaborative annotation.

For enterprise use it supports LDAP/ADS and OAuth2 authentication, REST and SOAP APIs for embedding conferencing into other applications, calendar-based meeting scheduling, and granular moderator permission controls.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Openmeetings is entirely free under the Apache License 2.0, with no licensing fees, per-user costs, or paid tiers offered by the project itself.

Organizations self-host it on their own infrastructure or through third-party hosting providers, and can optionally engage independent consultancies for commercial support since the ASF does not sell support contracts.

features.title: HTML5 video conferencing
features.description: Audio and video meetings powered by the Kurento media server, accessible from web browsers without plugins.

features.title: Screen sharing
features.description: Share screens with adjustable quality levels during meetings.

features.title: Multi-whiteboard collaboration
features.description: Multiple collaborative whiteboards per room with support for importing documents to annotate.

features.title: Meeting recording
features.description: Record meetings and export them as MP4 files with integrated playback.

features.title: Calendar and scheduling
features.description: Built-in calendar for scheduling meetings with automatic invitations.

features.title: Enterprise authentication
features.description: LDAP/ADS integration and OAuth2 support for enterprise identity management.

features.title: REST and SOAP APIs
features.description: Embed Openmeetings conferencing into external websites, intranets, or applications.

features.title: Polls and moderator controls
features.description: Run polls and voting with visualized results, plus granular permission controls for moderators.

pros: Completely free and open source under the Apache License
Self-hostable for full control over meeting data and privacy
No per-user licensing fees regardless of meeting size
HTML5-based with no plugin requirements
Integrates with enterprise identity systems via LDAP and OAuth2
cons: User interface is dated compared to modern tools like Zoom or Google Meet
Self-hosting requires server and media infrastructure expertise (Kurento setup)
Smaller community and slower release cadence than commercial competitors
Mobile experience is more limited than native mobile conferencing apps
Documentation can be sparse for advanced configuration scenarios

faqs.question: Is Apache Openmeetings free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Openmeetings is free and open source under the Apache License 2.0, with no licensing costs from the project itself.

faqs.question: Can Openmeetings replace Zoom or Google Meet?
faqs.answer: It can for organizations willing to self-host and manage their own server infrastructure, though its interface and polish are less refined than mainstream commercial tools.

faqs.question: What media server does Openmeetings use?
faqs.answer: Openmeetings relies on the Kurento media server for real-time audio and video processing.

faqs.question: Does Openmeetings support meeting recording?
faqs.answer: Yes, meetings can be recorded and exported as MP4 files with built-in playback.

faqs.question: Can I integrate single sign-on with Openmeetings?
faqs.answer: Yes, Openmeetings supports LDAP/ADS authentication and OAuth2 for enterprise identity integration.

faqs.question: Is hosted Openmeetings available, or must I self-host?
faqs.answer: The Apache Software Foundation does not offer official hosting; organizations self-host it or use third-party hosting providers.

faqs.question: When did Openmeetings become an Apache top-level project?
faqs.answer: Openmeetings entered the Apache Incubator in December 2011 and became a top-level Apache Software Foundation project in December 2012.

use_cases.title: Online education and training
use_cases.description: Educational institutions host online classes and training sessions using Openmeetings' whiteboard and recording features.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted enterprise conferencing
use_cases.description: Enterprises that require full data control deploy Openmeetings on their own infrastructure instead of using SaaS conferencing tools.

use_cases.title: Embedded white-label conferencing
use_cases.description: Developers use Openmeetings' REST and SOAP APIs to embed video conferencing into their own websites or products.

enrichment.short_description: OpenMeter is a usage metering and billing infrastructure platform for AI and API-based products, offering an open-source self-hosted engine and a managed cloud service now operated as Kong Metering and Billing after Kong's 2025 acquisition.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (open-source, self-hosted); managed cloud pricing is custom via Kong sales
enrichment.company_size: Now part of Kong Inc. (acquired September 2025); originally an 11-50 employee startup
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California, USA (now a subsidiary of Kong Inc.)
enrichment.seo_title: OpenMeter Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenMeter is open-source usage metering and billing infrastructure for AI and API products, now part of Kong. See features, pricing, and alternatives for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OpenMeter is a usage metering and billing infrastructure platform designed for developers building AI, API, and infrastructure products that need usage-based pricing. It was founded in 2023 by Peter Marton and Andras Toth, went through Y Combinator's Winter 2023 batch, and raised roughly 3.12 million dollars in seed funding from investors including Haystack, Sunflower Capital, STRT Holding, and Y Combinator, with headquarters in San Francisco, California. Marton previously worked on Stripe's usage and cost platform and built serverless infrastructure at Netflix, and earlier co-founded RisingStack, a Node.js monitoring company, giving OpenMeter's founding team direct experience with usage-based billing at scale. On September 3, 2025, OpenMeter was acquired by Kong Inc., the API infrastructure company, to power AI and API monetization; the product has since been positioned as 'Kong Metering and Billing' within Kong's Konnect platform, while the underlying open-source metering engine remains available on GitHub under an Apache 2.0 license for self-hosted use. OpenMeter's core capability is converting raw events, logs, and metrics into real-time usage records that can be billed, rate-limited, or used to enforce quotas, including edge-based limit enforcement so usage caps can be applied without added latency. It provides a no-code product catalog for iterating on pricing plans, credits, and commitments without new engineering work, plus revenue insight dashboards that surface expansion and churn signals for sales and finance teams. Developers integrate it via SDKs for TypeScript, Python, and Go, and it connects to existing observability and infrastructure tooling including OpenTelemetry, Kubernetes, LangChain, and PostgreSQL. OpenMeter is particularly suited to AI companies that need to bill customers per token or per API call, SaaS platforms transitioning to usage-based pricing, and infrastructure providers metering compute or storage consumption. Its main alternatives include Metronome, Lago, Orb, and Stripe Billing's usage-based features, with OpenMeter's key differentiators being its open-source self-hosted option and, post-acquisition, deep integration with Kong's existing API gateway and Konnect customer base for companies already using Kong for API management.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenMeter is usage metering and billing infrastructure for AI, API, and infrastructure products, letting developers turn raw usage events into billable records and enforce quotas in real time.

Founded in 2023 in San Francisco and backed by Y Combinator, OpenMeter was acquired by Kong Inc. in September 2025 and now operates as Kong Metering and Billing, while its open-source metering engine remains available for self-hosting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: OpenMeter ingests events, logs, and metrics and converts them into real-time usage records, supporting edge-based quota enforcement and per-token billing for AI workloads.

A no-code product catalog lets teams change pricing plans, credits, and commitments without engineering work, and SDKs for TypeScript, Python, and Go integrate it into existing OpenTelemetry, Kubernetes, and PostgreSQL-based stacks.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core OpenMeter engine is open source under the Apache 2.0 license and free to self-host.
The managed cloud service, now branded Kong Metering and Billing, is priced on a custom basis through Kong's sales team rather than published self-serve tiers.

features.title: Real-time usage metering
features.description: Converts events, logs, and metrics into real-time, billable usage records.

features.title: Edge-based quota enforcement
features.description: Enforces usage limits at the edge to control costs without adding latency to requests.

features.title: No-code pricing catalog
features.description: Change pricing plans, credits, and commitments without new engineering work.

features.title: Per-token AI billing
features.description: Purpose-built support for billing AI and LLM usage on a per-token or per-call basis.

features.title: Revenue insight dashboards
features.description: Surface expansion opportunities and churn risk based on customer usage patterns.

features.title: Multi-language SDKs
features.description: Official SDKs for TypeScript, Python, and Go for straightforward integration.

features.title: OpenTelemetry and Kubernetes integration
features.description: Connects to existing observability and infrastructure pipelines via OpenTelemetry and Kubernetes.

pros: Purpose-built for AI and API usage-based billing use cases
Open-source, self-hosted option avoids vendor lock-in
Backed by Kong's enterprise API infrastructure since the 2025 acquisition
Developer-friendly SDKs across TypeScript, Python, and Go
No-code pricing catalog speeds up plan and pricing changes
cons: Managed cloud pricing now requires contacting Kong sales, reducing transparency
Product direction is shifting toward Kong's Konnect ecosystem post-acquisition
Open-source self-hosted community is smaller than dedicated billing platforms like Stripe Billing
Still a relatively new product with limited long-term track record at massive scale
Recent rebrand to Kong Metering and Billing may cause confusion for existing OpenMeter users

faqs.question: Is OpenMeter still an independent company?
faqs.answer: No, OpenMeter was acquired by Kong Inc. in September 2025 and now operates as Kong Metering and Billing, though its open-source engine remains available.

faqs.question: Is OpenMeter free to use?
faqs.answer: The open-source metering engine is free to self-host under the Apache 2.0 license; the managed cloud service is custom-priced through Kong.

faqs.question: What programming languages does OpenMeter support?
faqs.answer: OpenMeter offers official SDKs for TypeScript, Python, and Go.

faqs.question: Can I self-host OpenMeter?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core metering engine is open source and can be self-hosted independently of Kong's managed cloud offering.

faqs.question: What is Kong Metering and Billing?
faqs.answer: It is the rebranded managed cloud version of OpenMeter, offered within Kong's Konnect platform following Kong's 2025 acquisition of OpenMeter.

faqs.question: Who is OpenMeter designed for?
faqs.answer: It is designed for AI companies, API providers, and SaaS platforms that need to implement usage-based billing and metering.

faqs.question: When was OpenMeter founded?
faqs.answer: OpenMeter was founded in 2023 by Peter Marton and Andras Toth and went through Y Combinator's Winter 2023 batch.

use_cases.title: AI and LLM usage billing
use_cases.description: AI companies use OpenMeter to bill customers per token or per API call for LLM-powered products.

use_cases.title: SaaS usage-based pricing
use_cases.description: SaaS platforms implement usage-based or hybrid pricing models using OpenMeter's metering and quota infrastructure.

use_cases.title: Infrastructure and API metering
use_cases.description: Infrastructure providers meter compute, storage, or API consumption to support consumption-based pricing.

features: SDKs for TypeScript, Python, and Go

enrichment.short_description: OpenNebula is an open-source cloud computing and IaaS platform for building and managing private, public, hybrid, and edge clouds, backed by Madrid-based OpenNebula Systems with a free Community Edition and paid Enterprise subscriptions.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Community Edition); Enterprise subscriptions are custom-priced
enrichment.headquarters: Madrid, Spain (La Finca Business Park), with a US subsidiary in Cambridge, Massachusetts
enrichment.seo_title: OpenNebula Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenNebula is an open-source cloud and edge infrastructure management platform. See its features, Community vs Enterprise pricing, and alternatives for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OpenNebula is an open-source cloud computing platform for managing private, public, hybrid, and edge computing infrastructure at data-center scale. The project was first released in 2008, and its founding developers established the company C12G Labs in Madrid, Spain, in March 2010 to provide commercial services around it; the company renamed itself OpenNebula Systems in September 2014 to align more closely with the product. OpenNebula Systems created a US subsidiary, OpenNebula Systems USA LLC, in Cambridge, Massachusetts in April 2015, and moved its Madrid headquarters to La Finca Business Park in November 2019. The company now employs roughly 50 people across Europe, North America, and Asia. OpenNebula's technical core combines support for KVM virtual machines, LXC system containers, and AWS Firecracker microVMs with CNCF-certified Kubernetes clusters (via its OneKE service built on SUSE Rancher), plus multi-tenancy, federation, and automatic provisioning across data centers, public clouds, and edge locations. More than 5,000 organizations run OpenNebula clouds worldwide, including large federated environments spanning hundreds of thousands of CPU cores, positioning it as a lighter-weight, European-rooted alternative to OpenStack for organizations that want data sovereignty and simpler operations than a full OpenStack deployment. The product is offered under an open-source Community Edition free to download and self-host, and a paid Enterprise Edition available through Standard and Premium subscription tiers; Standard includes enterprise tooling, 9x5 CET/EST SLA-backed support, unlimited CPUs/cores/VMs, and two named support accounts, while Premium adds 24x7 support, four named accounts, remote SSH access for OpenNebula engineers, and supervised upgrade assistance. Enterprise pricing is not published and requires contacting OpenNebula's sales team for a tailored quote; the company also offers embedded OS subscriptions (Ubuntu Pro, RHEL, SLES) and add-ons like mission-critical support, technical account managers, and Kubernetes/RKE2 support. OpenNebula targets telecom and service providers building private and edge clouds, research institutions and universities running self-managed HPC or cloud infrastructure, and enterprises seeking an OpenStack alternative for data center virtualization, particularly in Europe where data sovereignty is a priority. Its primary competitors are OpenStack, Proxmox VE, VMware vSphere/Cloud Foundation, and public hyperscalers for workloads that do not require self-hosted infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenNebula is an open-source cloud computing platform for building and managing private, public, hybrid, and edge cloud infrastructure, first released in 2008 and backed commercially by Madrid-based OpenNebula Systems since 2010.

It combines KVM, LXC, and Firecracker virtualization support with CNCF-certified Kubernetes clusters, and is used by more than 5,000 organizations worldwide as a lighter-weight alternative to OpenStack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: OpenNebula supports KVM virtual machines, LXC system containers, and AWS Firecracker microVMs alongside CNCF-certified Kubernetes clusters via its OneKE service built on SUSE Rancher.

It provides multi-tenant federation and automatic provisioning of cloud and edge resources, letting organizations manage data center, public cloud, and edge infrastructure through a single control plane.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Community Edition is free and open source under an Apache 2.0-style license, suitable for organizations comfortable with self-support and community forums.

The Enterprise Edition is available in Standard and Premium subscription tiers, both custom-priced, offering SLA-backed support ranging from 9x5 to 24x7, additional named support accounts, and supervised upgrade assistance.

features.title: Multi-hypervisor virtualization
features.description: Supports KVM virtual machines, LXC system containers, and AWS Firecracker microVMs.

features.title: Kubernetes integration
features.description: Deploy CNCF-certified Kubernetes clusters via the OneKE service built on SUSE Rancher.

features.title: Cloud and edge federation
features.description: Manage private, public, hybrid, and edge infrastructure resources from a single federated control plane.

features.title: Multi-tenancy
features.description: Isolate and manage resources for multiple tenants or departments within one OpenNebula deployment.

features.title: Self-service cloud portal
features.description: Give end users a self-service portal to provision virtual machines and containers within defined limits.

features.title: Automatic provisioning
features.description: Automate provisioning of compute, storage, and networking resources across infrastructure.

features.title: Built-in monitoring and chargeback
features.description: Track resource usage for monitoring and internal chargeback purposes.

pros: Fully open-source core with no mandatory vendor lock-in
Lighter-weight and simpler to operate than OpenStack
Strong support for hybrid and edge cloud federation at scale
Proven at scale with 5,000-plus organizations and hundreds of thousands of managed cores
Appeals to European organizations prioritizing data sovereignty and on-premises control
cons: Smaller ecosystem and community than OpenStack
Enterprise pricing is not published and requires a sales conversation
Steeper learning curve for teams new to infrastructure-as-a-service concepts
Fewer third-party integrations than major hyperscaler-native tools
Premium support tiers can be costly for small teams

faqs.question: Is OpenNebula free?
faqs.answer: The Community Edition is free and open source; the Enterprise Edition with SLA-backed support is a paid, custom-priced subscription.

faqs.question: How does OpenNebula compare to OpenStack?
faqs.answer: OpenNebula is generally considered lighter-weight and simpler to operate than OpenStack, while OpenStack has a larger ecosystem and community.

faqs.question: Does OpenNebula support Kubernetes?
faqs.answer: Yes, OpenNebula can deploy CNCF-certified Kubernetes clusters through its OneKE service, built on SUSE Rancher.

faqs.question: Who uses OpenNebula?
faqs.answer: More than 5,000 organizations worldwide use OpenNebula, including telecom providers, research institutions, and enterprises running private and edge clouds.

faqs.question: What is the OpenNebula Enterprise Edition?
faqs.answer: It is a paid subscription tier (available as Standard or Premium) that adds SLA-backed support, additional named accounts, and enterprise tooling on top of the open-source core.

faqs.question: Can OpenNebula run edge infrastructure?
faqs.answer: Yes, OpenNebula supports federation and automatic provisioning across data center, public cloud, and edge computing resources.

faqs.question: Where is OpenNebula Systems headquartered?
faqs.answer: OpenNebula Systems is headquartered in Madrid, Spain, with a US subsidiary based in Cambridge, Massachusetts.

use_cases.title: Telecom and service provider clouds
use_cases.description: Telecom operators build private and edge clouds on OpenNebula to manage distributed infrastructure at scale.

use_cases.title: Research and HPC infrastructure
use_cases.description: Universities and research institutions use OpenNebula to self-manage HPC and cloud infrastructure without hyperscaler dependence.

use_cases.title: OpenStack alternative for data center virtualization
use_cases.description: Enterprises seeking a lighter-weight alternative to OpenStack use OpenNebula for private cloud and data center virtualization.

enrichment.short_description: OpenObserve is an open-source observability platform unifying logs, metrics, traces, and real user monitoring, built as a cost-effective, cloud-native alternative to Elasticsearch and Datadog with up to 140x lower storage costs.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted open source); Cloud from $0.50/GB ingested
enrichment.seo_title: OpenObserve Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenObserve is an open-source observability platform for logs, metrics, and traces with low storage costs. Compare features, pricing, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OpenObserve is an open-source, cloud-native observability platform that unifies logs, metrics, traces, real user monitoring (RUM), session replay, and error tracking in a single system. The company was founded in 2022 by CEO Prabhat Sharma and is headquartered in Menlo Park, California. It has raised a total of about 13.6 million dollars, including a 10 million dollar Series A round led by Nexus Venture Partners and Dell Technologies Capital, both of whom had previously backed the company's seed round. As of 2026 the company employs roughly 30 people and reports that more than 6,000 organizations, including many Fortune 100 companies, actively run its open-source platform. OpenObserve's core differentiator is dramatically lower storage costs than incumbent observability tools like Elasticsearch and Datadog, which it attributes to a built-in compression architecture built on object storage that eliminates the need for separate database management, claiming up to 140 times lower storage costs than Elasticsearch. The platform supports both SQL and PromQL querying and offers AI-powered observability features including an AI SRE agent for incident management and an AI assistant, currently offered free during a preview period with complimentary usage credits. OpenObserve is distributed under multiple deployment and pricing models: a perpetually free, self-hosted open-source edition; a self-hosted Enterprise edition that is free for up to 50 GB of daily ingestion (with SSO, RBAC, federated search, audit trail, and sensitive data redaction), requiring a sales conversation above that threshold; and a Cloud Professional pay-as-you-go tier priced at 0.50 dollars per GB ingested (with a 30 percent discount for annual commitments) and 0.01 dollars per GB queried, including a 14-day free trial, unlimited users with no per-seat charges, SSO included at no extra cost, and default retention of 15 months for metrics and 30 days for logs, traces, and RUM data (extendable at 0.02 dollars per GB per additional 30-day period). A custom-priced Cloud Enterprise tier adds extended retention, data pipelines, sensitive data redaction, incident management, an AI SRE agent, an AI assistant, audit trails, unlimited users, SSO and RBAC, premium support, and volume discounts with SLAs. OpenObserve targets engineering and SRE teams looking to replace costly Elasticsearch- or Datadog-based logging and monitoring stacks, compliance-driven enterprises that need self-hosted observability with audit trails, and teams that want AI-assisted incident response built into their observability platform. Its main competitors include Elasticsearch/Elastic Observability, Datadog, Grafana Loki/Grafana Cloud, Splunk, and other emerging low-cost observability platforms like SigNoz and Better Stack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenObserve is an open-source, cloud-native observability platform unifying logs, metrics, traces, real user monitoring, session replay, and error tracking, founded in 2022 and headquartered in Menlo Park, California.

Its main pitch is dramatically lower storage costs than incumbent tools like Elasticsearch and Datadog, achieved through a compression-focused architecture built on object storage that eliminates separate database management.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: OpenObserve unifies logs, metrics, traces, RUM, session replay, and error tracking in a single platform queryable with both SQL and PromQL.

AI-powered features, including an AI SRE agent for incident management and an AI assistant, are currently offered free during a preview period, alongside enterprise features like SSO, RBAC, federated search, and audit trails.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: OpenObserve offers a perpetually free, self-hosted open-source edition, and a self-hosted Enterprise edition that remains free for up to 50 GB of daily ingestion.

Its Cloud Professional tier is pay-as-you-go at 0.50 dollars per GB ingested and 0.01 dollars per GB queried, with unlimited users and no per-seat charges, while a custom-priced Cloud Enterprise tier adds extended retention, AI features, and premium support.

features.title: Unified observability data
features.description: Combines logs, metrics, traces, real user monitoring, session replay, and error tracking in one platform.

features.title: Low-cost storage architecture
features.description: Built-in compression on object storage delivers up to 140x lower storage costs compared to Elasticsearch.

features.title: SQL and PromQL querying
features.description: Query observability data using familiar SQL or PromQL syntax.

features.title: AI SRE agent
features.description: AI-assisted incident management agent to accelerate root-cause analysis, free during preview.

features.title: Zero database management
features.description: Runs on object storage without requiring separate database administration.

features.title: Data pipelines and redaction
features.description: Enterprise pipelines for data transformation and sensitive data redaction.

features.title: SSO, RBAC, and audit trail
features.description: Enterprise-grade access control, single sign-on, and audit logging for compliance-driven teams.

pros: Significantly lower storage costs than Elasticsearch or Datadog
Unifies logs, metrics, traces, and RUM in a single platform
Generous free self-hosted Enterprise tier up to 50GB per day
Backed by a strong Series A and adoption from Fortune 100 companies
Simple, transparent cloud pricing with no per-seat charges
cons: Younger platform with a less mature ecosystem than Elastic or Datadog
Advanced AI features are still in preview
Enterprise features like SSO and audit trail require a paid tier at scale
Smaller third-party integration marketplace than established APM vendors
Self-hosted Enterprise scaling beyond 50GB/day requires custom sales negotiation

faqs.question: Is OpenObserve free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the self-hosted open-source edition is free perpetually, and the self-hosted Enterprise edition is free for up to 50GB of daily ingestion.

faqs.question: How does OpenObserve compare to Elasticsearch or Datadog?
faqs.answer: OpenObserve claims up to 140x lower storage costs than Elasticsearch by using a compression-focused architecture on object storage, while also unifying logs, metrics, and traces in one platform.

faqs.question: What data types does OpenObserve support?
faqs.answer: It supports logs, metrics, traces, real user monitoring, session replay, and error tracking in a single platform.

faqs.question: Does OpenObserve require a separate database?
faqs.answer: No, OpenObserve runs on object storage and does not require separate database management.

faqs.question: What is OpenObserve's AI SRE agent?
faqs.answer: It is an AI-powered incident management feature that helps accelerate root-cause analysis, currently offered free during a preview period.

faqs.question: Can I self-host OpenObserve at large scale?
faqs.answer: Yes, though the free self-hosted Enterprise tier covers up to 50GB per day; beyond that, pricing requires contacting OpenObserve's sales team.

faqs.question: How much does OpenObserve Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: Cloud Professional is pay-as-you-go at $0.50 per GB ingested and $0.01 per GB queried, with a 30 percent discount for annual commitments; Cloud Enterprise is custom-priced.

use_cases.title: Replacing costly Elasticsearch or Datadog stacks
use_cases.description: Engineering teams migrate to OpenObserve to cut logging and monitoring storage costs significantly.

use_cases.title: AI-assisted incident response
use_cases.description: SRE teams use OpenObserve's AI SRE agent and AI assistant to speed up incident detection and root-cause analysis.

use_cases.title: Compliance-driven self-hosted observability
use_cases.description: Regulated enterprises self-host OpenObserve to keep observability data in-house while meeting audit trail and access control requirements.

features: Incident management and AI SRE agent
